medications.indication_rationale: A sublingual/orally-disintegrating antiemetic that dissolves under the tongue and is absorbed without needing to be swallowed and retained — appropriate first-line control of the intractable vomiting when the patient cannot keep anything down. Because it is a prescription medicine and the patient cannot tolerate oral intake, in telehealth the AI Doctor cannot itself administer or prescribe it; the correct action is to arrange in-person review where it can be given, and to explain it as the expected treatment. The dose below is the dose the source clinician used.

medications.drug_class: sedating H1 antihistamine with antiemetic/vestibular-suppressant action
medications.indication_rationale: A vestibular-suppressant antiemetic for ongoing nausea and dizziness once vomiting is controlled enough to retain oral medication; a standard agent for motion sickness. It is a prescription medicine, so in telehealth it is part of the plan arranged at in-person review rather than administered by the AI Doctor. The dose below is the dose the source clinician prescribed.
medications.dose_route_frequency: promethazine 25 mg orally every 6 to 8 hours as needed
medications.duration: short-term, as needed until the vertigo and nausea settle
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Additive sedation with alcohol or other CNS depressants — advise against driving or operating machinery while taking it (relevant given the patient lives alone).

medications.indication_rationale: Careful sip-based oral rehydration is essential to counter the fluid loss, but only once the vomiting is controlled; taken as tiny amounts (a teaspoon every few minutes) to avoid distending the stomach and re-triggering vomiting. If oral intake still cannot be tolerated, parenteral (IV) rehydration at the in-person assessment is required.
medications.dose_route_frequency: one teaspoon (~5 mL) of an electrolyte solution every 5 minutes, increasing slowly as tolerated

medications.indication_rationale: The Epley manoeuvre treats benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, not kinetosis; it is not indicated here and, as the source noted, is not attempted during active severe vomiting because provoking head movement worsens the emesis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Lie flat on your back and keep your head as still as possible; avoid sudden head turns, and get up slowly and only when steadier.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Head movement re-stimulates the vestibular apparatus and re-triggers the vertigo and vomiting; minimising motion allows the sensory conflict to settle.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rehydrate with disciplined tiny sips — one teaspoon of electrolyte fluid every 5 minutes — rather than gulping a whole glass.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Small volumes are absorbed without distending the stomach and stretching the receptors that provoke vomiting, so fluid is retained where a full glass would be brought back up.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the room cool and well ventilated and avoid strong odours and stuffy heat.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Heat, stuffiness and strong smells worsen nausea; a cool, fresh environment reduces the emetic stimulus.

patient_education_points.point: This kind of severe seasickness happens because your inner ear sensed the boat's motion while your eyes saw a still cabin — the brain's confusion between those signals sets off a powerful reflex to vomit. The unsteady 'ground still moving' feeling can linger for 12 to 24 hours after you're back on land.

A wafer antiemetic that dissolves under the tongue works even when you can't keep anything down, because it is absorbed without needing to be swallowed — this is why an in-person visit to give it is worthwhile if you still can't hold fluids.

Because severe dizziness and vomiting can occasionally be caused by a problem in the brain rather than the inner ear, it is important to be examined in person today so a clinician can check your balance, eye movements and coordination.
Seek help straight away if you develop a sudden severe headache, weakness or numbness on one side, slurred speech, double vision, or if you cannot pass urine for more than 12 hours.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person assessment today; then GP review in about 48 hours if the dizziness persists or hydration cannot be maintained
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Focused neurological examination to exclude a central cause, hydration status and ability to tolerate oral fluids, and resolution of the vertigo; reconsider vestibular neuritis if symptoms persist beyond 24 hours of vestibular rest.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any neurological red flag (sudden severe headache, one-sided weakness/numbness, slurred speech, double vision, worsening unsteadiness), continued inability to keep fluids down, no urine output for over 12 hours, or vomiting blood.

must_include_items: Recognise a likely benign vestibular cause (severe motion sickness) from the clear kinetic trigger and autonomic prodrome
Actively screen for central-cause red flags (severe headache, focal weakness/numbness, dysarthria, diplopia, worsening unsteadiness) and acknowledge a cerebellar stroke as the can't-miss mimic
Recognise that a central cause cannot be excluded remotely and arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment for a neurological examination and a retainable (sublingual/parenteral) antiemetic
Address the dehydration risk — advise teaspoon-sip electrolyte rehydration and flag that IV rehydration may be needed if oral intake is not tolerated
Give head-still / minimise-movement symptom advice and safety-net for the stroke and dehydration red flags
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to an after-hours home-visit service or urgent care rather than a GP clinic, given the patient cannot travel safely while vertiginous
Advising a sublingual/wafer antiemetic obtained via an in-person prescriber as the route around the oral intolerance
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for central-cause (stroke) red flags before reassuring
Confirming benign motion sickness remotely and discharging without arranging any in-person examination
Ignoring the inability to tolerate oral fluids and the dehydration risk
Giving no safety-netting for the stroke or dehydration red flags
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a stable patient with no focal deficits and a clear kinetic trigger to an ambulance (000) or emergency department
Advising or attempting an Epley/repositioning manoeuvre, which treats BPPV not kinetosis and provokes vomiting
Recommending only an oral antiemetic and oral fluids as the whole plan when the patient cannot keep anything down
Advising the patient to drive themselves anywhere while acutely vertiginous
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising the likely benign vestibular cause AND arranging urgent same-day in-person assessment for the unexcludable central mimic and the dehydration, with red-flag safety-netting. Confirming benign motion sickness remotely with no in-person plan (under-triage) or an ambulance/ED response with no red flags (over-triage) are both failing responses.
